title: 第 28 節
en: It is not my object in these lectures either to defend or to attack any form of religion : there is no lack of hands for either the one or the other task. My own work, as I have traced it out for myself, and as it seemed to be traced out for me by the spirit of the founder of these lectures, is totally different. It is historical and psychological. Let theologians, be they Brahmanas or $rama^as, Mobeds or Mollahs, Eabbis or Doctors of Divinity, try to deter mine whether any given religion be perfect or im perfect, true or false ; what we want to know is, THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 219 how religion is possible ; how human beings, such as we are, came to have any religion at all ; what religion is, and how it came to be what it is.

When we are engaged in the science of language, our first object is, not to find out whether one language is more perfect than another, whether one contains more anomalous nouns or miraculous verbs than another. We do not start with a conviction that in the beginning there was one language only, or that there is at present, or that there will be in the future, one only that deserves to be called a language. No : we simply collect facts, classify them, try to understand them, and thus hope to discover more and more the real antecedents of all language, the laws which govern the growth and decay of human speech, and the goal to which all language tends.

It is the same with the science of religion. Each of us may have his own feeling as to his own mother-tongue, or his own mother-religion ; but as historians we must allow the same treatment to all. We have simply to collect all the evidence that can be found on the history of religion all over the world, to sift and classify it, and thus to try to discover the necessary antecedents of all faith, the laws which govern the growth and decay of human religion, and the god to which all religion tends. Whether there ever can be one perfect uni versal religion, is a question as difficult to answer as whether there ever can be one perfect universal language. If we can only learn that even the most imperfect religion, like the most imperfect language, is something beyond all conception wonderful, we shall have learnt a lesson which is worth many a lesson in the various schools of theology.

It is a very old saying, that we never know a thing, unless we know its beginnings. We may know a great deal about religion, we may have read many of the sacred books, the creeds, the catechisms, and liturgies of the world, and yet religion itself may be something entirely beyond our grasp, unless we are able to trace it back to the deepest sources from whence it springs.

In doing this, in trying to discover the living and natural springs of religion, we must take nothing for granted, except what is granted us by all philosophers, whether positive or negative. I explained in my first lecture, how I was quite prepared to accept their terms, and I mean to keep to these terms to the very end of my course. We were told that all knowledge, in order to be know ledge, must pass through two gates and two gates only : the gate of the senses, and the gate of reason. Religious knowledge also, whether true or false, must have passed through these two gates. At these two gates therefore we take our stand. What ever claims to have entered in by any other gate, whether that gate be called primeval revelation or religious instinct, must be rejected as contraband of thought ; and whatever claims to have entered by the gate of reason, without having first passed through the gate of the senses, must equally be rejected, as without sufficient warrant, or ordered at least to go back to the first gate, in order to produce there its full credentials.

Having accepted these conditions, I made it the chief object of my lectures to lay hold of religious ideas on their passing for the first time through the gates of our senses; or, in other words, I tried to THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 221 find out what were the sensuous and material beginnings of those ideas which constitute the prin cipal elements of religious thought.

I endeavoured to show, first of all, that the idea of the infinite, which is at the root of all religious thought, is not simply evolved by reason out of nothing, but supplied to us, in its original form, by our senses. If the idea of the infinite had no sensuous percept to rely on, we should, according to the terms of our agreement, have to reject it. It would not be enough to say with Sir W. Hamilton, that the idea of the infinite is a logical necessity ; that we are so made that wherever we place the boundary of space or time, we are conscious of space and time beyond. I do not deny that there is truth in all this, but I feel bound to admit that our opponents are not obliged to accept such reasoning.

I therefore tried to show that beyond, behind, beneath, and within the finite, the infinite is always present to our senses. It presses upon us, it grows upon us from every side. What we call finite in space and time, in form and word, is nothing but a veil or a net which we ourselves have thrown over the infinite. The finite by itself, without the infinite, is simply inconceivable ; as inconceivable as the infinite without the finite. As reason deals with the finite materials, supplied to us by our senses, faith, or whatever else we like to call it, deals with the infinite that underlies the finite. What we call sense, reason, and faith are three functions of one and the same perceptive self: but without sense, both reason and faith are impossible, at least to human beings like ourselves.
The history of the ancient religion of India, so far 222 LECTURE V.

as we have hitherto been able to trace it, is to us a history of the various attempts at naming the in finite that hides itself behind the veil of the finite. We saw how the ancient Aryans of India, the poets of the Veda, first faced the invisible, the unknown, or the infinite in trees, mountains and rivers ; in the dawn and the sun ; in the fire, the storm-wind, and the thunder ; — how they ascribed to all of them a self, a substance, a divine support, or whatever else we like to call it ; and how, in doing so, they always felt the presence of something which they could not see behind what they could see, of something super natural behind the natural, of something super-finite or infinite behind or within the finite. The names which they gave, the nomina, may have been wrong : but the search itself after the numina was legitimate. At all events, we saw how that search led the ancient Aryans as far as it has led most amongst ourselves, viz. to the recognition of a Father which is in heaven. Nay, we shall see that it led them further still. The idea that God is not a father, then, like a father, and lastly a father, appears in the Veda at a very early time. In the very first hymn of the Rig- Veda, which is addressed to Agni, we read : ' Be kind to us, as a father to his son/ The same idea occurs again and again in the Vedic hymns. Thus we read, Big- Veda I, 104, 9, ' Hear us, Indra, like a father ! ' In III, 49, 3 the poet says that Indra gives food, hears our call, and is kind to us, like a father. In VII, 54, 2, Indra is asked to be kind, as a father to his sons. Again, Rig- Veda VIII, 21, 14, we read: ' When thou thunderest and gatherest the clouds, then thou art called like a father.' Rig- Veda X, 33, 3, ' As mice eat their tails, sorrows eat me up, THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 223 me thy worshipper, all-powerful god ! For once, 0 mighty Indra, be gracious to us ! Be to us like a father!' Rig- Veda X, 69, 10, 'Thou borest him as a, father bears his son in his lap.' Rig- Veda III, 53, 2, ' As a son lays hold of his father by his skirt, I lay hold of thee by this sweetest song/ In fact, there are few nations who do not apply to their god or gods the name of Father.

But though it was a comfort to the early Aryans in the childhood of their faith, as it is to us in the faith oi our childhood, to call God father, they soon per ceived that this too was a human name, and that like all human names, it said but little, compared with what it was meant to say. We may envy our ancient forefathers, as we envy a child that lives and dies full of faith that he is going from one home to another home, from one father to another father. But as every child grows up to learn that his father is but a child, the son of another father ; as many a child, on becoming a man, has to surrender one idea after another that seemed to form the verv essence of father, so the ancients learnt, and we all of us have to learn it, that we must take out of that word father one predicate after another, all in fact that is con ceivable in it, if we wish to apply it still to God. So far as the word is applicable to man, it is inappli cable to God ; so far as it is applicable to God, it is inapplicable to man. 'Call no man your father upon the earth : for one is your Father, which is in heaven/ Matt, xxiii. 9. Comparison, as it began, so it often ends with negation. Father is, no doubt, a better name than fire, or the storm-wind, or the heaven, or the Lord, or any other name which man has tried to give to the infinite, that infinite of which he felt 224 LECTURE V.

the presence everywhere. But father too is but a weak human name, the best, it may be, which the poets of the Veda could find, but yet as far from him whom they were feeling after, as the east is from the west.

Having watched the searchings of the ancient Aryans after the infinite in every part of nature, and having tried to understand the names which they gave to it, beginning with trees and rivers and mountains, and ending with their Heavenfather, we have now to consider the origin of some other ideas which, at first, might seem completely beyond the reach of our senses, but which never theless can be shown to have had their deepest roots and their true beginnings in that finite or natural world which, it is difficult to say why, we are so apt to despise, while it has been everywhere and is still the only royal road that leads us on from the finite to the infinite, from the natural to the supernatural, from nature to nature's God.
Theogony of the Veda.

By imagining ourselves placed suddenly in the midst of this marvellous world, we tried to find out what would be the objects most likely to have startled, to have fascinated, to have awed our earliest forefathers — what would have roused and awakened them from mere staring and stolid wonderment, and have set them for the first time musing, pondering, and thinking on the visions floating past their eyes. And having done that, we tried to verify our anticipations by comparing notes with the poets of the Veda, in whose songs the most ancient records of religious thought are preserved to us, at least so THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 225 far as that branch of humanity is concerned to which we ourselves belong. No doubt, between the first daybreak of human thought and the first hymns of praise, composed in the most perfect metre and the most polished language, there may be, nay there must be, a gap that can only be measured by gene rations, by hundreds, aye by thousands of years. Yet such is the continuity of human thought, if once controlled by human language, that, on carefully examining the Vedic hymns, we found most of our anticipations realised, far beyond what we had any right to expect. The very objects which we had singled out as most likely to impress the mind with the sense that they were something more than what could be seen, or heard, or felt in them, had really served, if we might trust the Veda, as ' the windows through which the ancient Aryans first looked into infinitude.'
The infinite in its earliest conception.

When I say infinitude, do not let us take the infinite in its quantitative sense only, as the infinitely small or the infinitely great. Though this is perhaps the most general concept of the infinite, yet it is at the same time the poorest and emptiest. To the ancient Aryans the aspect of the infinite varied with the aspect of each finite object of which it, the infinite, was the ever present background or com plement. The more there was of the visible or audible or tangible or finite, the less there was of the invisible, the inaudible, the intangible, or the infinite in the consciousness of man. As the reach of the senses varied, so varied the suspicion of what might be beyond their reach.

The concept, for instance, of a river or a mountain would require far less of invisible background than the concept of the dawn or the storm-wind. The dawn approaches every morning, but what it is, and whence it comes, no one can tell. ' The wind bloweth where it listeth, and thou hearest the sound thereof, but canst not tell whence it cometh and whither it goeth/ It was easy to understand the ravages caused by the inundation of a river or by the fall of a mountain ; it was more difficult to understand what causes the trees to bend before the approach of a hurricane, and who it is that, during a dark thunderstorm, breaks asunder the mountains and overthrows the stables and huts.

The so-called semi-deities therefore, which always remained to a great extent within the reach of the senses, seldom assumed that dramatic character which distinguishes other deities ; and among those deities again, those who were entirely invisible, and had nothing in nature to represent them, such as Indra, the rainer, Eudra, the howler, the Maruts, the pounders or storm-gods, even Varu?m, the allembracer, would soon assume a far more personal and mythological aspect than the bright sky, the dawn, or the sun. Again, what constitutes the infinite or supernatural character of all these beings, would at once be clothed in a simply human form. They would not be called infinite, but rather inconquerable, imperishable, undecaying, immortal, un born, present everywhere, knowing everything, achieving everything, and at the very last only should we expect for them names of so abstract a nature as infinite.

I say, we should expect this, but I must say THE IDEAS OF INFINITY AND LAW. 227 at the same time, that this expecting attitude is often very dangerous. In exploring new strata of thought, it is always best to expect nothing, but simply to collect facts, to accept what we find, and to try to digest it.
Aditi, the infinite.

You will be surprised, for instance, as I certainly was surprised when the fact first presented itself to me, that there really is a deity in the Veda who is simply called the boundless or the infinite, in Sanskrit A-diti.

Aditi is derived from diti, and the negative particle a. Diti again is regularly derived from a root DA (dyati), to bind, from which dita, the participle, meaning bound, and diti, a substantive, meaning binding and bond. Aditi therefore must originally have meant without bonds, not chained or enclosed, boundless, infinite, infinitude. The same root shows itself in Greek Sew, I bind, SiaSwa, a diadem, that is bound round the head. The sub stantive diti would in Greek be represented by Sea-is, a-diti by d-Sean?.

It is easy to say that a deity, having such a name as Aditi, the infinite, must be of late origin. It is much wiser to try to learn what is, than to imagine what must be. Because the purely abstract concept of the infinite seemed modern, several of our most learned Vedic students have at once put down Aditi as a late abstraction, a.s being invented simply to account for the name of her sons, the well-known Adityas or solar deities. From the fact that there are no hymns entirely addressed to her, they have concluded that Aditi, Q 2 228 LECTURE V.
as a goddess, came in at the very last moments of Vedic poetry.

The same might be said of Dyaus, a name corre sponding with the Greek ZeJ?. He occurs even less frequently than Aditi amongst the deities to whom long hymns are addressed in the Veda. But so far from being a modern invention, we know now that he existed before a word of Sanskrit was spoken in India, or a word of Greek in Greece ; that he is in fact one of the oldest Aryan deities, who at a later time was crowded out, if I may use that expres sion, by Indra, Kudra, Agni and other purely Indian gods.
Aditi not a modern deity.

The same, I believe, is the case with Aditi. Her name occurs in invocations together with Dyaus, the sky, Pr/thivi, the earth, Sindhu, the rivers, and other really primitive deities ; and far from being a purely hypothetical mother of the Adityas, she is represented as the mother of all the gods.

In order to understand this, we must try to find out what her own birthplace was, what could have suggested the name of Aditi, the boundless, the in finite, and what was the visible portion in nature to which that name was originally attached.
Natural origin of Aditi.
zh: 我在這些講座中的目標，既非辯護亦非攻擊任何形式的宗教：這兩項任務都不乏人手。我為自己所規劃的工作，以及這些講座創辦者的精神似乎為我所規劃的工作，全然不同。它是歷史性的與心理學的。讓神學家們——無論是婆羅門（Brahmana）或沙門（Śramaṇa）、穆貝德（Mobed）或毛拉（Mollah）、拉比（Rabbi）或神學博士（Doctor of Divinity）——去判定某一特定宗教是完美或不完美、真實或虛假；我們想要知道的是，宗教如何成為可能；像我們這樣的人類，究竟如何會擁有任何宗教；宗教是什麼，以及它如何成為它現在這個樣子。
當我們從事語言科學時，首要目標並非去判定某一種語言是否比另一種更完美，或某一種語言是否比另一種包含更多不規則名詞或奇異動詞。我們並非一開始就抱持著這樣的信念：起初只有一種語言，或者現在、未來也只有一種語言配得上稱為語言。不：我們只是單純地收集事實、加以分類、試圖理解它們，並希望藉此逐步發現所有語言的真正前身、支配人類言語興衰的法則，以及所有語言所趨向的終極目標。

宗教學亦是如此。我們每個人對自己的母語或自己的母教，或許各有各的感受；但作為歷史學家，我們必須對待一切宗教一視同仁。我們只需收集全世界宗教史中所有可得的證據，加以篩選、分類，從而試圖發現一切信仰的必要前因、支配人類宗教興衰的法則，以及一切宗教所趨向的神。究竟能否存在一種完美無缺的普世宗教，這個問題之難回答，正如究竟能否存在一種完美無缺的普世語言。倘若我們能學到，即使最不完美的宗教，也如同最不完美的語言一樣，是超乎一切想像的奇妙之物，那麼我們所學到的這一課，便勝過神學各學派中的許多課程。
這是一句非常古老的格言：除非我們知道一件事物的起源，否則我們永遠無法真正認識它。我們可能對宗教知之甚詳，可能讀過世上許多聖典、信經、教理問答與禮儀書，然而，除非我們能夠追溯宗教至其最深邃的源頭，否則宗教本身對我們而言，可能仍是完全無法掌握的。

在進行這項工作、試圖發現宗教那活潑而自然的源頭時，我們不可預設任何前提，除非是所有哲學家——無論是實證派或否定派——都同意給予我們的前提。我在第一講中已說明，我完全準備好接受他們的條件，並且打算在我的課程中始終堅守這些條件。我們被告知：一切知識，若要成為知識，必須通過兩道門，而且僅此兩道門：感官之門與理性之門。宗教知識亦然，無論其為真為假，都必曾通過這兩道門。因此，我們便在這兩道門前站定立場。凡聲稱經由其他門戶進入者——無論那門戶被稱為原始啟示或宗教本能——都必須被當作思想的違禁品而予以拒絕；凡聲稱經由理性之門進入，卻未先通過感官之門者，同樣必須被拒絕，因其缺乏充分憑證，或者至少應被要求返回第一道門，以便在那裡出示其完整的資格證明。
接受這些條件之後，我將講座的主要目標設定為：把握宗教觀念首次穿越我們感官之門的瞬間；換言之，我試圖找出那些構成宗教思想主要元素的觀念，其感性與物質的開端究竟為何。

我首先試圖證明，作為一切宗教思想根源的「無限者」觀念，並非單純由理性憑空推演而出，而是透過感官以其原始形式提供給我們。倘若無限者的觀念沒有感官知覺作為依據，那麼按照我們約定的條件，就必須予以摒棄。僅以漢彌爾頓爵士（Sir W. Hamilton）的說法——即無限者的觀念是一種邏輯必然性；我們天生如此，無論在空間或時間的邊界置於何處，都會意識到邊界之外仍有空間與時間——來論證，是不夠充分的。我並不否認其中包含真理，但我必須承認，我們的對手沒有義務接受這類推論。

因此，我試圖說明：在有限者之外、之後、之下與之內，無限者始終向我們的感官呈現。它向我們迫近，從四面八方籠罩我們。我們在空間與時間、形式與言詞中所謂的有限者，不過是我們自己拋向無限者的一層面紗或一張網。有限者若無無限者，根本無法設想；正如無限者若無有限者，同樣無法設想。當理性處理感官提供給我們的有限材料時，信仰——或我們樂意稱之的任何名稱——則處理作為有限者基礎的無限者。我們所謂的感官、理性與信仰，乃是同一個感知自我的三種功能；但若無感官，理性與信仰皆不可能——至少對像我們這樣的人類而言是如此。

印度古代宗教的歷史，就目前所知，始於《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）的讚歌。這些讚歌是雅利安人（Aryan）最古老的文學遺產，他們在遷徙至印度之前，曾與波斯人共同生活於同一地區。在這些讚歌中，我們發現了一種宗教，它既非多神教（polytheism），亦非一神教（monotheism），而是被稱為「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）的形態。這種宗教的特點在於，當某位神祇被呼喚時，祂便成為至高無上的存在，其他神祇則暫時退居次要地位。這種現象在吠陀（Veda）宗教中尤為顯著，例如對阿耆尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）或伐樓那（Varuna）的讚歌中，每位神祇都被賦予了絕對的權能與榮耀。穆勒（Müller）指出，這種宗教思維的根源在於人類對「無限者」（the Infinite）的直覺感知，而這種感知又與語言的本質密切相關。他認為，神話的起源往往源於語言的誤解或隱喻的具體化，即所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。因此，比較神話學（comparative mythology）與比較神學（comparative theology）必須藉助語言科學（the science of language）的方法，才能揭示古代宗教的真正意涵。在吠陀文獻中，我們可以看到特尤斯（Dyaus）作為天父的形象，以及提婆（Deva）作為神聖存在的稱謂，這些詞彙都與希臘文的Ζεύς（Zeus）和拉丁文的Deus同源，顯示了雅利安語族共同的宗教遺產。然而，隨著時間的推移，這種原始的單一神教逐漸退化為多神教，甚至偶像崇拜，而奧義書（Upanishad）則試圖回歸對唯一實相的哲學探求。穆勒強調，宗教學（the science of religion）的任務不僅在於描述這些歷史現象，更在於理解人類宗教意識的普遍發展規律。

就我們迄今所能追溯的而言，對我們來說，這是一部關於為隱藏在有限面紗背後的無限者命名的各種嘗試的歷史。我們看到，古印度的雅利安人，即吠陀的詩人們，最初是如何面對樹木、山巒與河流中的不可見者、未知者或無限者；在黎明與太陽中；在火焰、暴風與雷電中——他們如何將自我、實體、神聖支撐，或任何我們樂於稱呼的名稱，歸於這一切；而在這樣做的過程中，他們始終感受到某種他們在可見之物背後所看不見之物的臨在，某種自然背後超自然之物的臨在，某種有限背後或之內超有限或無限之物的臨在。他們所給出的名稱（nomina）或許是錯誤的：但對神靈（numina）本身的追尋卻是正當的。無論如何，我們看到這項追尋引領古雅利安人走得有多遠，正如它引領我們大多數人一樣，即達到了對一位在天之父的認識。不僅如此，我們還將看到它引領他們走得更遠。上帝不是一位父親，然後像一位父親，最終就是一位父親——這種觀念在吠陀中出現得非常早。在《梨俱吠陀》的第一首頌詩中，這首獻給阿耆尼的詩，我們讀到：「請善待我們，如同父親對待兒子。」同樣的觀念在吠陀頌詩中反覆出現。例如，我們讀到《梨俱吠陀》I, 104, 9：「聽聞我們吧，因陀羅，如同一位父親！」在III, 49, 3中，詩人說因陀羅賜予食物，聽聞我們的呼喚，並善待我們，如同父親。在VII, 54, 2中，因陀羅被請求要仁慈，如同父親對待兒子。再次，《梨俱吠陀》VIII, 21, 14，我們讀到：「當你雷鳴並聚集雲朵時，你便被稱為如同父親。」《梨俱吠陀》X, 33, 3：「如同老鼠啃食自己的尾巴，憂傷吞噬著我，*無限與法則之觀念*。223 我，你的崇拜者，全能之神！哦，強大的因陀羅，請這一次對我們施恩！請對我們如同父親！」《梨俱吠陀》X, 69, 10：「你懷抱著他，如同父親將兒子抱在膝上。」《梨俱吠陀》III, 53, 2：「如同兒子抓住父親的衣角，我藉由這首最甜美的歌抓住你。」事實上，很少有民族不將「父親」這個名稱應用於他們的神或眾神。

然而，雖然在早期雅利安人信仰的童年時期，稱呼上帝為父親是一種安慰，正如對我們而言，在我們童年的信仰中也是如此，他們很快就察覺到，這也只是一個人類的名稱，而且如同所有人類的名稱一樣，與它原本試圖表達的意涵相比，它所傳達的實在微乎其微。我們或許會羨慕我們古老的先祖，如同我們羨慕一個孩子，他終其一生都懷抱著堅定的信念，相信自己正從一個家前往另一個家，從一位父親前往另一位父親。但是，正如每個孩子長大後都會明白，他的父親也只不過是個孩子，是另一位父親的兒子；正如許多孩子在成為大人時，不得不放棄一個又一個看似構成「父親」本質的概念，古人學到了，而我們所有人都必須學到：如果我們仍想將「父親」這個詞應用於上帝，就必須從中逐一剝除所有的屬性，事實上，就是剝除這個詞語中所有可被理解的部分。就這個詞語適用於人類而言，它便不適用於上帝；就它適用於上帝而言，它便不適用於人類。「不要稱呼地上的人為父，因為只有一位是你們的父，就是天上的父。」（馬太福音 23:9）。比較，如同它開始時那樣，也常常以否定告終。「父親」無疑是一個比「火」、「暴風」、「蒼天」、「主」，或任何人類試圖賦予無限者——這個他所感受到的無限者——的任何其他名稱更好的名稱。
無所不在。但「父親」也不過是一個脆弱的人類稱呼，或許是吠陀詩人所能找到的最佳詞彙，然而它與他們所追尋的那一位之間的距離，仍如東方之於西方般遙遠。

我們已觀察古代雅利安人在自然萬物中追尋無限者的歷程，也試圖理解他們為此所賦予的名稱——從樹木、河流、山巒起始，最終歸於其天父。如今，我們必須審視其他一些觀唸的起源；這些觀念乍看之下，似乎完全超出感官所能觸及的範圍，然而仍可證明，它們最深的根源與真正的開端，正是植基於那個有限而自然的世界。我們不知為何，總是輕易鄙棄這個世界，但它卻始終——至今依然——是唯一一條引領我們從有限通往無限、從自然通往超自然、從自然通往自然之神的康莊大道。

在吠陀詩歌中，我們發現了神話學的雛形，這些雛形後來在印度史詩與《往世書》中發展為成熟的神譜體系。然而，即便在《梨俱吠陀》最古老的讚歌裡，我們也能觀察到一種趨向，即把眾多神祇歸結為少數幾個主要人物，這些人物逐漸被賦予更高層次的神性，最終幾乎達到一神教的境界。但這種一神教並非始終如一；它更像是一種輪換主神教（kathenotheism），即每一位神祇在受到祈禱時，都被視為至高無上的存在。這種現象在《梨俱吠陀》中尤為顯著，例如對阿耆尼（Agni）、因陀羅（Indra）或伐樓那（Varuna）的讚歌，皆將該神祇讚頌為唯一且全能的主宰。然而，這種對單一神祇的專注，並未排除其他神祇的存在；它僅是暫時性地將其他神祇置於陰影之中。這種宗教思維的獨特階段，穆勒稱之為單一神教（henotheism），以有別於嚴格的一神教（monotheism）與多神教（polytheism）。吠陀詩人似乎從未完全滿足於任何一種固定的神學體系；他們的神祇如同流動的雲彩，不斷變換形狀與位置。這種不穩定性，部分源於語言本身的力量——即所謂「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）——它使神話隱喻逐漸被視為實體存在。例如，天父「特尤斯」（Dyaus）這個詞，原本僅指明亮的天空，後來卻人格化為一位神祇；同樣地，「提婆」（Deva）一詞，原意為「發光者」，後來也成為神靈的通稱。透過比較神話學（comparative mythology）的研究，我們得以追溯這些詞彙從自然現象到神話人物的演變過程，從而揭示宗教學（the science of religion）的深層結構。

藉由想像自己驟然置身於這奇妙世界的中心，我們試圖找出哪些對象最可能驚動、迷魅、震懾我們最早的先祖——哪些事物能將他們從單純的凝視與呆滯的驚奇中喚醒，並首次促使他們沉思、默想、思索眼前飄逝的景象。完成這項工作後，我們試圖藉由比對《吠陀》詩人的記錄來驗證我們的預期；在這些詩歌中，宗教思想最古老的紀錄得以保存給我們——至少就我們自身所屬的人類分支而言是如此。無疑地，在人類思想最初的曙光與以最完美韻律和最精煉語言譜寫的首批讚美詩之間，可能存在著——不，必然存在著——一道只能以世代、以數百年、甚至數千年來衡量的鴻溝。然而，人類思想一旦為人類語言所掌控，其連續性竟如此強大，以致當我們仔細審視《吠陀》讚歌時，發現我們大部分的預期都已實現，遠超出我們合理期待的範圍。我們所挑選出的那些最可能打動心靈、使其意識到其中存在著超越眼見、耳聞或觸感之物的對象，若我們能信賴《吠陀》的記載，確實已作為「窗戶，古代雅利安人透過它們首次凝視無限之境。」

> “There is a law which runs through nearly all the phenomena of nature, and all the departments of human thought—the law that the human mind can only conceive the finite, but can only believe in the infinite.” > 「有一條法則貫穿幾乎所有自然現象與人類思想的各個領域——即人類心智只能構想有限者，卻只能信仰無限者。」 若我們想理解宗教的起源，就必須追問：人類如何首次觸及那超越有限感官的「無限者」（the Infinite）？在最早的文獻中，例如《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda），我們發現一種對自然力量的驚嘆——從黎明（Ushas）到蒼穹（Dyaus），這些力量起初並非被視為個別的神祇，而是作為無限者的隱約顯現。雅利安人（Aryan）的早期祈禱中，並未將天空或太陽視為有限之物，而是視為那不可見、不可觸及的「無限者」的象徵。這種直覺並非來自推理，而是來自一種本能——一種對「超越」的原始感知，正如語言科學（the science of language）所揭示的：每個詞根最初都指向某種感官經驗，但當這些詞被用來指稱神聖時，它們便超越了自身，成為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）的開端——即隱喻被誤認為實體。 因此，最早的宗教形式並非多神教（polytheism）或一神教（monotheism），而是一種「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism）：在每一次祈禱中，所呼喚的神明被視為至高無上，其他神祇暫時隱退。這種現象在《吠陀》（Veda）中尤為明顯——無論是特尤斯（Dyaus）、提婆（Deva），還是阿耆尼（Agni），每一位都在其被頌讚的時刻成為無限者的化身。這並非邏輯的產物，而是情感與想像的必然結果：人類在面對浩瀚自然時，本能地將有限經驗投射向無限，從而誕生了宗教學（the science of religion）所研究的第一個對象——那尚未被命名的「無限者」。

當我說「無限」時，請不要僅從量的意義來理解無限，將其視為無窮小或無窮大。雖然這或許是「無限」最普遍的概念，但同時也是最貧乏、最空洞的。對古代的雅利安人而言，無限者的樣貌隨著每一個有限對象的樣貌而變化——無限者正是這些有限對象始終存在的背景或補充。在人類的意識中，可見、可聞、可觸或有限的事物愈多，不可見、不可聞、不可觸或無限的事物就愈少。感官所能及的範圍如何變化，人們對感官所不及之物的揣測也隨之變化。
例如，河流或山脈的概念所需的不見背景，遠少於黎明或暴風的概念。黎明每日清晨降臨，但它究竟是什麼、從何而來，無人能道。『風隨意而吹，你聽見風的響聲，卻不曉得從哪裡來，往哪裡去。』河流氾濫或山崩所造成的破壞不難理解；但要明白是什麼力量使樹木在颶風來襲前彎腰，以及在昏暗的雷雨夜裡，是誰劈開山巒、掀翻馬廄與茅舍，則更為困難。

因此，所謂的半神祇，在很大程度上始終停留在感官可及的範圍內，很少具備那種區分其他神祇的戲劇性特質；而在那些神祇之中，那些完全不可見、在自然界中沒有任何事物能代表他們的神祇——例如因陀羅（Indra），降雨者；樓陀羅（Rudra），怒吼者；摩錄多（Maruts），撞擊者或風暴之神；甚至伐樓那（Varuṇa），無所不包者——很快就會比明亮的天空、黎明或太陽，展現出更為人格化與神話學的面貌。再者，所有這些存在所具有的無限或超自然特質，也會立即被披上單純的人類形式。他們不會被稱為「無限者」，而是被稱為不可征服者、不滅者、不衰者、不朽者、未生者、無所不在者、無所不知者、無所不能者；直到最後，我們才會預期他們擁有像「無限」這般抽象性質的名稱。
我說，我們理應預期如此，但同時也必須指出，這種預期心態往往非常危險。在探索思想的新層面時，最好的做法始終是無所預期，僅僅收集事實、接受所見，並設法加以消化。

> 在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中，阿底提（Aditi）被描述為「無限者」（the Infinite），或更確切地說，是「不受束縛者」（the unbounded）。她並非僅是眾神之母，而是某種更為深遠的存在——她是那無邊無際、無所不包的領域，從中湧現出所有存在形式。當吠陀詩人呼喚阿底提時，他們所觸及的，正是那超越一切有限界定的神聖根基。此一概念，與我們在宗教學（the science of religion）中所追蹤的「無限者」（the Infinite）之覺醒，有著直接的關聯。正如語言科學（the science of language）揭示出「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）如何導致神話的誕生，我們在此也看到，從對無限者的原初直觀中，如何分化出單一神教（henotheism）、輪換主神教（kathenotheism），乃至多神教（polytheism）與一神教（monotheism）的種種形態。雅利安（Aryan）民族的宗教歷程，從對特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）的崇敬，到奧義書（Upanishad）中對阿底提的哲學沉思，正是一段逐步澄清「無限者」意涵的歷史。而閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的傳統，則以不同的路徑，回應著同一終極奧秘。
您會感到驚訝，舉例來說，正如這個事實首次呈現在我面前時，我確實也感到驚訝：吠陀中確實有一位神祇，祂的名字在梵文中就叫做「無邊者」或「無限者」，即 A-diti。

Aditi 源自 diti，加上否定詞綴 a。而 diti 又規則地派生自詞根 DA（dyati），意為「束縛」，由此產生分詞 dita，意為「被束縛的」，以及名詞 diti，意為「束縛」與「枷鎖」。因此 Aditi 最初必然意指「無束縛」、「不受桎梏或限制」、「無邊無際」、「無限」、「無限性」。同一詞根亦顯現於希臘文 δέω（我束縛）、διάδημα（王冠，即束於頭部之物）。名詞 diti 在希臘文中對應為 δέσις，而 a-diti 則對應為 ἄδεσις。

輕易便能斷言，一位名為阿底提（Aditi）——即「無限者」——的神祇，必定是晚近才出現的。然而，試圖去認識事物「是什麼」，遠比憑空想像事物「必定是什麼」來得明智。正因為「無限者」這個純粹抽象的概念看似現代，我們幾位最淵博的吠陀學者便立刻將阿底提歸為晚出的抽象概念，認為祂不過是為瞭解釋其子嗣——即著名的阿底提耶（Ādityas）或太陽神祇——之名而被創造出來的。從沒有任何頌歌是完全獻給祂的事實，他們推斷阿底提……
作為一位女神，她在吠陀詩歌的最後時刻才出現。

特尤斯（Dyaus）這個名稱與希臘文的Ζεύς相對應，也可作如是觀。在吠陀中那些獻給諸神、篇幅漫長的頌詩裡，祂出現的頻率甚至比阿底提（Aditi）還要低。然而，祂絕非近代的發明；我們如今知道，早在印度有人說出任何一個梵文字詞、或希臘有人說出任何一個希臘字詞之前，祂便已存在。事實上，祂是雅利安人最古老的神祇之一，只是到了較晚的時期，才被因陀羅（Indra）、樓陀羅（Rudra）、阿耆尼（Agni）以及其他純粹印度神祇所排擠——若容我使用這個說法的話。
Aditi 並非一位現代神祇。
我相信阿底提（Aditi）的情況也是如此。她的名字與天父特尤斯（Dyaus）、大地波哩提毗（Pr/thivi）、河流信度（Sindhu）以及其他真正原始的諸神一同出現在祈禱文中；她絕非純屬假設的阿底提耶（Adityas）之母，而是被描繪為眾神之母。
為了理解這點，我們必須設法查明她自身的誕生之地為何，是什麼可能啟發了「阿底提」（Aditi）——即「無邊者」、「無限者」——這個名稱，以及在自然界中，這個名稱最初所依附的具體可見部分又是什麼。

然而，阿底提（Aditi）這個名字本身，其詞源學含義似乎指向一個更為自然的起源。在梵文中，*aditi* 意為「無限性」、「無邊無際」或「不受束縛」。它由否定前綴 *a-* 與詞根 *dā*（意為「束縛」、「捆綁」）結合而成，因此字面意義即為「無束縛」或「無限」。這個詞在《梨俱吠陀》中頻繁出現，不僅作為一位女神的名字，也作為一個抽象名詞，用以描述一種無邊無際、不受限制的狀態。這種從抽象概念到具體神祇的轉變，在宗教觀唸的演化中並非罕見。我們可以推測，阿底提最初可能只是用來形容蒼穹或大氣層那種無垠廣闊的性質，後來才被人格化為眾神之母。這種從「無限者」（the Infinite）這一抽象概念，過渡到一位具有生育能力的女性神祇的過程，恰好體現了神話思維中，將自然現象與抽象力量賦予人格特徵的典型模式。
